lots of commentary in here. Some of it, he did some things I really, really liked,

as he's doing it ... the thing to remember is this is a launch that did $450,000.
Nicholas started from scratch with basically no list. He's selling a product about
how to make art, which isn't considered one of those high dollar niches and yet he
did $450,000. You'll note there's no perfection here but he does a great job of just
delivering his message and being super authentic.
Now we're going to break down Nicholas Wilton's sales video. This is a sales
video for his Art2Life Program. He did a launch of $450,000. That was his first
launch and then he followed it up with a launch that was almost as big. There's a
lot of things that are right about this sales video. Not everything's perfect. I'm
going to point out some of the things I might have done differently. I'm going to
point out some of the things he did really, really well.
Again, Nicholas started off as a complete beginner with really no list, no online
marketing experience and he absolutely crushed this launch and it's going to be
fun to share this with you.
Now, one thing that I have to mention is that I love giving these case studies to
you because I think it's an absolute best way to teach and the best way for you to
learn. I give you lots of examples and also, this swipe vault we call it. The term
swipe well, it's sort of misleading even though that's what it's called. I want you to
think of this more as a reference or inspiration, not something that you just copy
verbatim. That is very uncool to just copy something verbatim. Think of it as a
template or as an example.
You can use it for inspiration. Use it for inspiration. Don't steal it. This is
Nicholas's hard work. Okay, I want to show you, one thing he does in this video
is, he's direct to camera here and I'm giving you the whole video so you can watch
the whole thing but then in here around the nine minute mark, he transitions
from what we call the direct to camera, into screen capture.
Nicholas: Let me give you a quick tour inside Art2Life, Creative Visionary Path portal. Let
me give you a tour now and go over and just give you a peek of what's inside and
the kinds of things that we're going to be involved in in the next 12 weeks. Here's
the inside of the Art2Life, Creative Visionary Path portal. You can see at the top
here, all these modules; path, process, color, texture.

Here's a little bit of social proof and community and then we'll just keep on

This is sort of, he gives a solution and the problem, the solution and the

This is interesting. “That was something I was just thinking about. That was
something that I was thinking about that I didn't really maybe talk about in the
first few videos. By the way, you can go back and watch those. They're still
available. That was just an idea that I was thinking about this morning when I
was coming over to the studio.” I love that because it makes ... remember, this is
your fourth video. You don't want, just because it's a sales video, to all of a
sudden be some crazy big formal thing. It's just the next video. You don't want to
deliver three videos and have them all feel like the three PLC videos, your
prelaunch videos all have one feel and then all of a sudden, once you go into the
offer video, into the sales video, it feels completely different. Your manners, your
delivery, your language, the look, you don't want it to feel all different all of a
sudden and that's what I love about this paragraph here.
"This is just something I was thinking about in those first few videos. This is just
an idea I had this morning as I was coming over the studio." I just love that. I
absolutely love it.

I know I'm moving quickly. You can pause this video and read everything. Take

Here, he's talking about more transformation and then even more

Nicholas really, really has a deep feeling about the importance of

hard to sell community. You can try but people don't usually buy
stuff for community. Once they get in and they understand how powerful a
community is, that's what sticks them. That's what stops them from refunding,
that's what keeps them renewing, that's what keeps them buying more products.
It is that community and that feeling to be part of something bigger than yourself.
It's huge but in terms of the upfront sale, it's pretty hard to sell community. I
might actually put the community piece deeper in this sales video, later in the
sales video after I've gotten them super excited about other things, then say, "Oh,
by the way, there's this community. Community's really awesome and that's
what's going to pull you up and drive you forward."

This, I really like quite a bit. This person invariably raises their hand for a couple

one more time? It made sense over there but it really doesn't make sense on what
I'm doing here."
Nick says, "This always cracks me up because it's not that you need to ask one

Down here he's basically taking features and turning them into

“Everyone's going to be working on a series of paintings.” I think this is a really

isn't you watching me talk about stuff. It's, I give you training, actual training and

your paintings."
"There's a lot here. It's one thing to watch and another thing to put it into

Then this, I just love it, "Tell them it's a super fun module. This doesn't have to be

Right here, I'm just noting, it's the end of the screen capture section.

speaking to one person, even though I'm probably going to have thousands of

people watch this video, maybe 10’s of thousands, maybe 100’s of thousands,
whatever, it doesn't matter, you're speaking to one person. When you record that
video and in the words you say. It could be, "As you might know," now we're
speaking to one person. Be careful of your languaging.
This says, "Compare." This is authority building and comparison. You're
building up the comparison between your mentorship program, the seven-month
mentorship and the workshops you've been running for 25 years so there's real
authority there.
Let's see. Right down here, we're doing a value comparison. A dollar comparison.
The cost of the actual program, $13,000, is not insignificant. Now here he's
talking about his workshops. When he's doing it with 10 or 12 people, it costs
$13,000. By the way, there's a wait list so you can't even get in right now. This is
classic reason why copy, is what we call it where you tell them the
reason for something. The reason why he created this online interactive
program is because there's a wait list. People can't get into this and oh, by the
way, it's $13,000. Now, the price of this program's only $1,997 or there's a six-
month payment plan for $397. That's awesome. This is the big revelation of price.
Now, in here ... when someone's watching this and they are now 17 minutes into
this fourth video and presumably, if they've watched the earlier videos, that took
them 70 or 80 minutes and now they're another 17 minutes in. At this point,
they're interested, otherwise they wouldn't be watching. When you drop a price ...
when I say drop I mean mention ... when you mention $13,000 they go, "Oh my
gosh, 13,000 bucks. I was hoping it was going to be $9.97." They had no idea
anything about pricing and now you dropped that big number.
There's tension there. When you drop a number like that, I often like to give it
some room for that tension to build, for it to breathe, for it to grow because as it
goes here, this is about what the timing will be. “The cost of the actual program,
$13,000, is not significant. It goes up every year. There is a wait list now in the
application process and everything. That's why I created this three-month
interactive online program. It's a fraction of the price. I can teach at any location.
I can reach anybody at any location at any time zone. The price for this program
is $1,997. We also offer a six-month payment plan of $397.” That's the timing of
it. You go straight from 13,000 and you drop it down to 1,997, which is what? One
sixth the price. I probably would have let that breathe just a little bit more,
another 30 seconds, put something else in between there and maybe it's this
paragraph that you put in between those two.

“You're going to be creating a mini series of paintings.” That sounds cool. “It
could be the best art you've ever made.” Even cooler. Something like that could go
right before the price revelation and you could come back and revisit something
like this right after the price revelation. You could mention it in two places.
This is interesting to me. “You can improve without painting, just by having more
understanding.” That's like a magic bullet. You don't even have to paint and
you're going to become a better painter. That is cool.
Here he mentions workshops in Spain, Mexico or Iceland. That's cool authority.
"Whether you're a beginning artist. This is for you if you're beginning, you're
coming back after a long time, or you're a professional and you're struggling." I
love it. I love it. You might want to put that right at the very top. Right in the intro
to this video. Oh, by the way, put that at the intro of PLC1 as well. When you're
telling people it's for photographers and sculptures and realistic painters tell
them it's also for beginning artists, coming back after years, and if you've just run
into a road block and you're a pro.
Here we're coming back to community and this is where I would expect to talk
about community, pretty deep in this page.
"I hope you can join us. We're going to start in a week and registration closes in
five days." This is the first mention of any deadline or any scarcity. You
could have put that earlier in this video. In fact, you could have mentioned that in
PLC3, right at the very end of PLC3, "I'm going to have a workshop. Make sure
you watch the next video as soon as I send it because I'm going to be talking
about my workshop and my registration is only going to be open for five days.
This program's not going to be offered for another year." That's scarcity.
"Go ahead and click the button." He didn't say click and join. Yeah. "Go ahead
and if you want to join, click on the registration button below." I love this because
you have to tell people what to do. When you get to this point, you can't just
assume they're going to scroll down, find a button and click the button. You have
to tell people what to do. You have to say, "Click the button and buy."
This is a bit of a take away sale. A take away sales like, "Yeah, it might not be right
for you. Why don't you just forget it?" When you do that, all of a sudden, people
want it more and so this is classic take away. "By the way, you're going to have to
do the work and if you don't want to do the work, I really don't want you."
